The global genetically modified crops market will grow from $21.08 billion in 2022 to $22.29 billion in 2023 at a compound annual growth rate (CAGR) of 5.8%. The Russia-Ukraine war disrupted the chances of global economic recovery from the COVID-19 pandemic, at least in the short term. The war between these two countries has led to economic sanctions on multiple countries, surge in commodity prices, and supply chain disruptions, causing inflation across goods and services effecting many markets across the globe. The genetically modified crops market is expected to grow to $28.03 billion in 2027 at a CAGR of 5.9%.  
  
The genetically modified crops market consists of revenues earned by entities that are engaged in producing genetically modified corn, genetically modified rice, and other genetically modified crops.The market value includes the value of related goods sold by the service provider or included within the service offering.  
  
Only goods and services traded between entities or sold to end consumers are included.  
  
Genetically modified crops (GM crops) are agricultural plants whose DNA has been altered using genetic engineering techniques.  
  
North America was the largest region in the genetically modified crop market in 2022.South America was the second largest region in the genetically modified crop market.  
  
The regions covered in the genetically modified crop market report are Asia-Pacific, Western Europe, Eastern Europe, North America, South America, Middle East, and Africa.  
  
The main trait types of genetically modified crops are herbicide tolerance (HT), insect resistance (IR), stacked traits (ST), and others.The main types of crops are soybeans, cotton, maize, oilseed rape, tobacco, and others.  
  
The techniques used in genetically modified crops are gene guns, electroporation, microinjection, agrobacterium, and other techniques. The types of modification are transgenic, cis-genic, sub-genic, and multiple trait integrations that are applied in scientific research and agricultural crops.  
  
Genetically modified (GM) crops have traits such as insect tolerance and herbicide tolerance that contribute to the high demand for such crops.Other benefits offered by genetically modified crops include high nutritional value, improved yield, and longer shelf life.  
  
For these reasons, farmers are resorting to GM crops to increase their yields and profits. According to the U.S. Department of Agriculture (USDA), genetically engineered insect-resistant cotton has helped to achieve a significant reduction in the use of synthetic pesticides that are contaminating groundwater and the environment. The environmental concerns and benefits offered by GM crops drive the demand for the market over the forecast period.  
  
The exponential increase in the world’s population around the globe drives the need to implement genetically modified crops.Farmers have to adopt new technologies to produce crops that can meet the demand of the growing population.  
  
GM crops help reduce food loss and waste, improve soil and water management, and improve the yield while protecting the environment.According to the UN, it is expected that the world population will be around 10 billion by 2050.  
  
Genetically modified crops are therefore important to feed this growing population and maintain food security.  
  
The stringent regulations imposed on the approval of GM crops limit the growth of the genetically modified crop market during the period.For instance, in India, only Bt cotton is permitted by the government for cultivation.  
  
Genetically modified brinjal was not approved by the environment ministry. Therefore, the stringent regulations imposed by the governments of various countries on the approval of GM crops impact the growth of the market.  
  
In October 2020, BASF SE, a Germany-based chemical manufacturing company, acquired ASL for an undisclosed amount.This acquisition was aimed at expanding the melon seed breeding capacities of BASF to secure the most inventive melon breeding research program and increase breeding and seed production in France.  
  
ASL is a France based seed production and breeding company that specializes in melons with orange flesh, such as the Cantaloupe and Charentais varieties.  
  
The countries covered in the genetically modified crop market are Australia, Brazil, China, France, Germany, India, Indonesia, Japan, Russia, South Korea, UK, and USA.

--- SOURCE 3: Consumers' perceptions and acceptance of genome ... ---
URL: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0963996924000528

SUMMARY:
This study aims to empirically investigate consumers' perceptions and acceptance of specific, beneficial agricultural applications of genome editing for food production in two countries with different regulatory backdrops (i.e., Switzerland and the United States of America). The literature on consumers' perceptions and acceptance of human intervention in plant breeding (e.g., Costa-Font et al., 2008, Siegrist, 2008, Siegrist and Hartmann, 2020) offers a solid basis for transferring methods, measurement instruments, and insights to the public's perception and acceptance of NGTs or genome editing. Siegrist How chemophobia affects public acceptance of pesticide use and biotechnology in agriculture  Food Quality and Preference, 91 (2021), Article 104197, 10.1016/j.foodqual.2021.104197 View PDFView articleView in ScopusGoogle Scholar

Consumers' perceptions and acceptance of genome editing in agriculture: Insights from the United States of America and Switzerland
==================================================================================================================================

Author links open overlay panel Angela Bearth a, Caitlin Drummond Otten b, Alex Segrè Cohen c

Show more

Outline

Add to Mendeley

Share

Cite

[https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodres.2024.113982](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodres.2024.113982 "Persistent link using digital object identifier")[Get rights and content](https://s100.copyright.com/AppDispatchServlet?publisherName=ELS&contentID=S0963996924000528&orderBeanReset=true)

Under a Creative Commons [license](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/)

Open access

Highlights
----------

*   •Consumers from the United States were more open towards NGTs than Swiss. 
*   •The regulatory context might have acted as a risk cue for consumers. 
*   •Acceptance was related to social trust, various values and personal preferences. 
*   •The organic sector’s presumptions about their customers’ views might be outdated. 

Abstract
--------

The terms “New Genomic Techniques” (NGTs) or “Genome Editing” refer to various methods that allow finding, cleaving, and repairing specific sequences in the genome. These techniques could contribute to managing various challenges in [plant breeding](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/plant-breeding "Learn more about plant breeding from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and [agriculture](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/agricultural-science "Learn more about agriculture from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Aside from regulatory uncertainties, the lack of consumer acceptance has frequently been cited as a significant barrier to the widespread use of NGTs in plant breeding and agriculture across the planet. This study was based on an anonymous online survey (N=1202). It investigated what consumers from two countries that differ in gene technology regulation, namely the United States of America and Switzerland, thought about three specific applications of NGTs in plant breeding (i.e., blight-resistant potato, gluten-free wheat, cold-resistant soybean). The study highlights the importance of the affect heuristic for acceptance, as half of the participants in both countries expressed positive feelings regarding the three applications, a quarter of the participants expressed negative, and the remaining participants expressed torn or neutral emotions. Some evidence was provided that the regulatory context might have acted as a risk cue, as participants in Switzerland expressed more negative feelings, perceptions, and lower acceptance than participants from the United States of America. Lastly, our findings underscore the importance of a collaboration between the life sciences and social sciences in balancing technological innovations and public perceptions and acceptance, which have been shown in this study to be impacted by affect, values, and context.

*   [Previous article in issue](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0963996924000541)
*   [Next article in issue](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0963996923014497)

Keywords
--------

Genome editing

New genomic techniques

Perception

Acceptance

Consumer behavior

1. Introduction and theoretical background
------------------------------------------

### 1.1. Background and research goal

There exists a broad consensus among researchers that techniques falling under the terms “New Genomic Techniques” (NGTs) or “Genome Editing” could, among other established and novel approaches, contribute to managing various challenges in [plant breeding](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/plant-breeding "Learn more about plant breeding from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and [agriculture](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/agricultural-science "Learn more about agriculture from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), such as droughts, pests, or extreme temperatures (e.g., [Kovak et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0963996924000528#b0145), [Lemarie and Marette, 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0963996924000528#b0165)). It has also been suggested that NGTs could offer benefits for [human nutrition](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/food-science/human-nutrition "Learn more about human nutrition from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), such as improving the nutritional content of foods or reducing allergenic components (e.g., [Lombardo and Grando, 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0963996924000528#b0175), [Tuberosa et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0963996924000528#b0315)). However, many of the benefits stated above were only investigated in laboratory or field trials and have yet to be tested in large-scale field trials or commercial implementations, which are challenged by the varying and evolving legal situations worldwide, ranging from complete bans (e.g., in Switzerland, EU member states) to similar regulations for conventional and NGT crops (e.g., in the United States of America, Japan, or recently also in the United Kingdom) ([Metje-Sprink et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0963996924000528#b0190), [Turnbull et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0963996924000528#b0320)). The legal situation in Europe might change, as the European Commission suggests exempting certain types of NGTs from the current ban (European [Commission, 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0963996924000528#b0075)).

Aside from regulatory uncertainties, the lack of consumer acceptance has frequently been cited as a major barrier to the widespread use of NGTs in plant breeding and agriculture ([Qaim, 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0963996924000528#b0230)). Consumers are concerned about the safety and environmental impacts of genetic modification, and these concerns may extend to NGTs (e.g., [Busch et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0963996924000528#b0055), [Costa-Font et al., 2008](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0963996924000528#b0070), [European Food Safety Authority, 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0963996924000528#b0080), [Siegrist, 2008](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0963996924000528#b0270)). From a scientific perspective, drawing a clear line between genetic modification and NGTs is challenging. In our article, the term “genetic modification” describes methods that involve introducing one or more genes from the same (cisgenic) or a different species (transgenic) into a crop, whereas “NGTs” refers to new methods that can but do not have to involve the transfer of genetic material from other species see [Lemarie and Marette (2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0963996924000528#b0165) for a similar differentiation).

NGTs may be perceived differently from older techniques, as the former is sometimes described as more precise than the latter and, thus, might be seen as less risky ([Bearth et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0963996924000528#b0035), [Nguyen et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0963996924000528#b0205)). Recent studies highlight that benefit perceptions may be more critical for the acceptance of new food technologies than risk perception ([Baum et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0963996924000528#b0030), [Bearth and Siegrist, 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0963996924000528#b0040), [Guo et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0963996924000528#b0110)). Technologies that offer a relevant benefit are more likely to be accepted than technologies that do not ([Bearth et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0963996924000528#b0035), [Saleh et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0963996924000528#b0245), [Segrè Cohen et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0963996924000528#b0260)), and potential risks directly or indirectly related to the technology are rated as more acceptable if a relevant benefit is offered ([Finucane et al., 2000](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0963996924000528#b0095)). A _meta_-analysis on the acceptance of new food technologies ([Bearth & Siegrist, 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0963996924000528#b0040)) speculated, based on an analysis of systematics in the heterogeneity in responses, that the regulation in a particular country might also impact public perceptions and acceptance of technology; for example, a complete ban might be taken as a warning sign or risk cue. This study aims to empirically investigate consumers' perceptions and acceptance of specific, beneficial agricultural applications of genome editing for food production in two countries with different regulatory backdrops (i.e., Switzerland and the United States of America).

### 1.2. Regulation in plant breeding and agriculture in different countries

Humans have optimised crops since the domestication of plants, increasing yield, improving resistance to diseases, or enhancing their nutritional and sensory attributes ([Schlegel, 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0963996924000528#b0255)). Since the introduction of Mendel's laws of inheritance, plant breeding has undergone several stages of innovation, ranging from crossbreeding to [mutagenesis](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/mutagenesis "Learn more about mutagenesis from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") to genetic modification to NGTs or genome editing ([Lee et al., 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0963996924000528#b0160), [Oladosu et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0963996924000528#b0210), [Schlegel, 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0963996924000528#b0255)). The overall goal of plant breeding is to breed plants with desirable qualities and to increase variety in the available crops. The term “conventional breeding” is used in most regions worldwide to describe crossbreeding and mutagenesis and distinguish these practices from “genetic modification.” However, many scientists argue that some changes made to crops through NGTs are indistinguishable from those produced through conventional breeding and even natural mutation ([Pacher and Puchta, 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0963996924000528#b0215), [Turnbull et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0963996924000528#b0320)). There have been calls from the global scientific community to overhaul the current regulations to focus on the crop and its genetic traits rather than on the process used to breed the crop ([Bradford et al., 2005](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0963996924000528#b0045), [Turnbull et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0963996924000528#b0320)).

Particularly in Europe, the emergence of NGTs has fostered regulatory debate about whether this technology should fall under the genetically modified organisms (GMOs) legislation (Regulation (EC) 1829/2003). This legislation requires a case-by-case risk assessment of specific applications of genetic modification before they can be authorised for commercial use in food or feed. Directive 2001/18/EC enables member states of the European Union to regulate the use and sale of GMOs within their own country. In Switzerland, the regulation is even stricter, with a temporary moratorium in place since 2005 that prohibits the cultivation and processing of GMO crops ([Turnbull et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0963996924000528#b0320)) aside from research purposes ([Romeis et al., 2013](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0963996924000528#b0240)). This moratorium has recently been prolonged until the end of 2025 ([Swiss Parliament, 2021a](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0963996924000528#b0305)). However, debates about exempting NGTs are underway in Europe ([European Commission, 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0963996924000528#b0075)) and Switzerland ([Swiss Parliament, 2021b](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0963996924000528#b0310)).

In the United States, the regulation of NGTs is primarily governed by the Coordinated Framework for Regulation of Biotechnology from 1986 (51 Fed. Reg. 23, 302), administered by three federal agencies: the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA), the Food and Drug Administration (FDA), and the United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) ([Turnbull et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0963996924000528#b0320)). Depending on the usage (e.g., food) and characteristics (e.g., does it contain DNA from “plant pests” like viruses or bacteria) of the crop, one or more of these agencies may be involved ([National Academies of Sciences, 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0963996924000528#b0200), [Waltz, 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0963996924000528#b0335)). For example, crops engineered to resist herbicides or browning may be regulated by the USDA's [Animal and Plant Health Inspection Service](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/animal-and-plant-health-inspection-service "Learn more about Animal and Plant Health Inspection Service from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (APHIS) under its authority to protect plant health by preventing [plant pests](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/plant-pests "Learn more about plant pests from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") ([Turnbull et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0963996924000528#b0320), [Wolt and Wolf, 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0963996924000528#b0340)). Crops either receive a regulated or a non-regulated status, which allows their cultivation without oversight from APHIS ([Turnbull et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0963996924000528#b0320), [Waltz, 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0963996924000528#b0335), [Wolt and Wolf, 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0963996924000528#b0340)). Overall, the Coordinated Framework for Regulation of Biotechnology signalled a shift from process-based regulation to more trait-based regulation ([Wolt & Wolf, 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0963996924000528#b0340)).

### 1.3. Public perception and acceptance of genome editing

The literature on consumers' perceptions and acceptance of human intervention in plant breeding (e.g., [Costa-Font et al., 2008](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0963996924000528#b0070), [Siegrist, 2008](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0963996924000528#b0270), [Siegrist and Hartmann, 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0963996924000528#b0285)) offers a solid basis for transferring methods, measurement instruments, and insights to the public's perception and acceptance of NGTs or genome editing. Recent articles suggest more positive views of NGTs than GMOs in countries like Switzerland and the United Kingdom ([Bearth et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0963996924000528#b0035)), Belgium and the Netherlands ([Ferrari et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0963996924000528#b0090)), Canada ([Muringai et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0963996924000528#b0195)), and the United States of America, Canada, Belgium, France, and Australia ([Shew et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0963996924000528#b0265)). However, translating these perceptions and acceptance into actual consumer behaviour depends on the context, particularly in countries with bans (e.g., available products and their attributes, regulatory frame, and media discourse). It is possible that the regulation and labelling of NGTs as GMOs will lower consumer acceptance ([Araki and Ishii, 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0963996924000528#b0015), [Asioli et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0963996924000528#b0020)) and that the availability of specific applications of NGTs and foods with societal or consumer benefits might shift perceptions and acceptance ([Bearth and Siegrist, 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0963996924000528#b0040), [Hakim et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0963996924000528#b0115), [Kanchiswamy et al., 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0963996924000528#b0135)).

The affect heuristic has been instrumental in explaining the public's reactions to (food) technologies ([Bearth and Siegrist, 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0963996924000528#b0040), [Finucane et al., 2000](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0963996924000528#b0095), [Fischer and Frewer, 2009](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0963996924000528#b0100), [Lerner et al., 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0963996924000528#b0170), [Peters and Slovic, 1996](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0963996924000528#b0220), [Siegrist, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0963996924000528#b0275)). The affect heuristic postulates that under uncertainty (i.e., complex issue, lack of expert knowledge), the public's risk and benefit perceptions and acceptance of novel technologies might be coloured by the spontaneous associations and affect raised by these associations ([Connor and Siegrist, 2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0963996924000528#b0065), [Lerner et al., 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0963996924000528#b0170), [Slovic et al., 2007](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0963996924000528#b0295)), leading risk and benefit judgments to be negatively correlated ([Connor and Siegrist, 2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0963996924000528#b0065), [Lerner et al., 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0963996924000528#b0170), [Slovic et al., 2007](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0963996924000528#b0295)). The following research question (RQ) was formulated for this study:
*   ●RQ1. What spontaneous associations and affective responses are raised by three different applications of genome editing in agriculture in consumers from the United States of America and Switzerland? 

Consumer perceptions and acceptance are linked to the specific purpose or benefit of the application of genome editing ([Bearth et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0963996924000528#b0035), [Busch et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0963996924000528#b0055), [Saleh et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0963996924000528#b0245)). Specifically, an application would be using genome editing or another NGT to achieve a specific goal or several goals in plant breeding. For example, [Saleh et al. (2021)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0963996924000528#b0245) found a higher acceptance of cisgenic genetic modification to make potatoes blight-resistant compared to the acceptance of synthetic or organic [fungicides](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/fungicide "Learn more about fungicides from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Similarly, [Busch et al. (2021)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0963996924000528#b0055) found that participants preferred applications that improve human health or enhance crop resistance over applications that change yield or [product quality](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/product-quality "Learn more about product quality from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Several authors ([Bearth and Siegrist, 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0963996924000528#b0040), [Busch et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0963996924000528#b0055), [Marette et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0963996924000528#b0185)) found differences in the public's evaluation of GMO or NGT applications across countries and, thus, regulatory frames. However, to our knowledge, authors have yet to explicitly investigate awareness of regulation and public perceptions and acceptance. The following research question and hypotheses (H) were formulated to address this in this study:
*   •

RQ2. How do consumers from the United States of America and Switzerland perceive three different applications of genome editing for food production in agriculture?
    *   o H2.1: The applications will be evaluated more positively in a country with more relaxed regulation (i.e., United States of America) compared to a country with more strict regulation (i.e., Switzerland) (main effect). 
    *   o H2.2: Applications with benefits associated with human health or plant resistance will be evaluated more positively than applications with benefits associated with yield (main effect). 
    *   o H2.3: Evaluation patterns for different applications will differ depending on the country and regulatory frame (interaction effect). 

Prior research has also focused on individual differences that may explain why some people actively or passively oppose GMOs and NGTs, whereas others accept them. As stated above, a regulatory ban on new technology, such as NGTs, might act as a risk cue ([Bearth & Siegrist, 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0963996924000528#b0040)). This would primarily depend on people's awareness or assumptions about the regulation in their own country (e.g., whether NGTs are banned), as not everyone might know the existing regulation. Based on the affect heuristic, the positive affect raised by the described application of NGTs would be associated with higher acceptance of this application. Next, trust is especially salient; higher trust in institutions and stakeholders, such as scientists, regulators, or industry, has been linked to more positive perceptions and acceptance ([Connor and Siegrist, 2010](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0963996924000528#b0060), [Hakim et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0963996924000528#b0115), [Siegrist, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0963996924000528#b0275), [Siegrist and Hartmann, 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0963996924000528#b0285)). ## [Report Description](#collapse-A)

## Genetically Modified Food Market Outlook

The global genetically modified (GM) food market size was valued at approximately USD 25 billion in 2023 and is projected to reach USD 50 billion by 2032, growing at a compound annual growth rate (CAGR) of 7.5% during the forecast period. The growth of the GM food market can be attributed to the increasing demand for high-yield crops, advancements in biotechnology, and supportive government policies in various developing and developed nations.

The rising global population is a significant driving factor for the GM food market. With the world's population expected to reach 9.7 billion by 2050, there is a pressing need to enhance agricultural productivity to ensure food security. Genetically modified foods, which have been engineered to improve yield, resistance to pests, and adaptability to various environmental conditions, offer a viable solution to meet this demand. Additionally, these crops often require fewer inputs such as water and pesticides, making them more sustainable and cost-effective for farmers.

Advancements in genetic engineering and biotechnology have also been pivotal in the expansion of the GM food market. Innovations such as CRISPR-Cas9 and other gene-editing technologies have made it easier and more cost-effective to develop GM crops with desired traits. These advancements have not only improved the efficiency of production but also expanded the range of traits that can be engineered. For instance, researchers are now able to develop crops that are not only resistant to pests and herbicides but also fortified with essential nutrients, thereby addressing issues of malnutrition in various parts of the world.

Another significant growth factor for the GM food market is the regulatory environment. Various countries have established frameworks to ensure the safety and efficacy of GM foods, thus building public trust and acceptance. For example, the United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) and the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) have stringent regulations that GM foods must meet before they can be commercialized. These regulations have helped to alleviate consumer concerns regarding the safety of GM foods and have paved the way for wider adoption.

In recent years, there has been a growing interest in [Non Gmo Corn Seed](https://dataintelo.com/report/non-gmo-corn-seed-market) as an alternative to genetically modified varieties. Non-GMO corn seeds are cultivated using traditional breeding methods, which do not involve genetic engineering. This approach appeals to consumers and farmers who are concerned about the potential environmental and health impacts of GMOs. Non-GMO corn is often perceived as a more natural option, aligning with the preferences of those seeking organic or minimally processed foods. The demand for Non-GMO corn seed is also driven by the increasing consumer awareness and desire for transparency in food production. As a result, some farmers are exploring the cultivation of Non-GMO corn to cater to niche markets and meet the diverse needs of consumers.

From a regional perspective, North America and Asia-Pacific are expected to be the leading markets for genetically modified foods. North America, particularly the United States, has been at the forefront of adopting GM crops due to favorable government policies and high investment in research and development. In contrast, Asia-Pacific is witnessing rapid adoption due to the need to enhance food security and agricultural productivity in densely populated countries such as China and India. Europe, however, has shown resistance due to stringent regulations and public skepticism, but there is a slow shift towards acceptance driven by ongoing research and positive outcomes from other regions.

## Crop Type Analysis

The genetically modified food market is segmented by crop type into corn, soybean, cotton, canola, and others. Among these, corn holds a significant share of the market due to its widespread use in food products, animal feed, and biofuels. Genetically modified corn is engineered to resist pests and tolerate herbicides, which increases its yield and reduces the need for chemical interventions. This has made it highly popular among farmers, particularly in the United States and Brazil, which are leading producers of GM corn.

Soybean is another dominant crop in the GM food market. Genetically modified soybeans are primarily designed for herbicide tolerance, which allows farmers to control weeds more effectively without damaging the crop. This has resulted in higher yields and lower production costs, making GM soybeans a preferred choice for farmers in countries like the United States, Argentina, and Brazil. Additionally, GM soybeans are extensively used in the production of animal feed, which further drives their demand.

Cotton is the third major crop in the genetically modified food market. GM cotton is engineered to be resistant to pests such as the bollworm, which has historically caused significant crop losses. The introduction of GM cotton has led to increased yields and reduced the need for chemical pesticides, thereby lowering the environmental impact of cotton farming. India and China are among the largest producers of GM cotton, contributing significantly to the global market.

Canola is another important crop in the genetically modified food market. GM canola is primarily engineered for herbicide tolerance, which helps in weed management and leads to higher yields. Canada is the largest producer of GM canola, and the crop is extensively used in the production of canola oil, which is a popular cooking oil. The growing demand for canola oil in various food products, coupled with the benefits of GM canola, is expected to drive the market further.

Other crops in the GM food market include fruits and vegetables like potatoes, tomatoes, and papayas, which have been genetically modified to resist diseases and improve shelf life. While these crops constitute a smaller segment compared to corn, soybean, cotton, and canola, they are gradually gaining acceptance as the benefits of genetic modification become more widely recognized.

## Trait Analysis

The genetically modified food market is also segmented by trait into herbicide tolerance, insect resistance, stacked traits, and others. Herbicide tolerance is one of the most common traits engineered into GM crops. Herbicide-tolerant crops can survive the application of specific herbicides that kill weeds, allowing for more efficient weed control. This trait is particularly beneficial for large-scale farming operations, where weed management is a significant challenge. It also reduces the need for tilling, which can help preserve soil structure and reduce erosion.

Insect resistance is another critical trait in the genetically modified food market. Crops with insect-resistant traits produce proteins that are toxic to specific pests but safe for human consumption and other non-target organisms. This reduces the need for chemical pesticides, which can have harmful environmental and health effects. Bt corn and Bt cotton are examples of insect-resistant GM crops that have been widely adopted around the world. The success of these crops has demonstrated the potential for genetically engineered insect resistance to improve crop yields and reduce environmental impacts.

Stacked traits refer to GM crops that possess multiple engineered traits, such as both herbicide tolerance and insect resistance. These crops offer the combined benefits of the individual traits, providing farmers with more comprehensive solutions to the challenges of pest and weed management. Stacked trait crops are particularly popular in regions with diverse agricultural challenges, as they offer a more versatile and effective approach to crop management. The adoption of stacked trait crops is expected to continue growing as more combinations of beneficial traits are developed.

Other traits in the genetically modified food market include drought tolerance, improved nutritional content, and disease resistance. Drought-tolerant crops are engineered to withstand periods of low water availability, which is increasingly important in the face of climate change and the growing scarcity of water resources. Crops with improved nutritional content are engineered to contain higher levels of essential nutrients, such as vitamins and minerals, addressing issues of malnutrition. Disease-resistant crops are engineered to be less susceptible to specific plant diseases, reducing the need for chemical treatments and improving yields.

The growing diversity of traits being engineered into GM crops highlights the potential for continued innovation in the genetically modified food market. As research and development efforts expand, it is likely that new traits will be discovered and commercialized, further enhancing the benefits of genetically modified crops and driving market growth.

## Application Analysis

The application of genetically modified foods extends across various sectors, including food, feed, industrial, and others. The food sector is the most significant application segment, as GM crops are used extensively in the production of various food products. For example, genetically modified corn and soybeans are used in processed foods, cooking oils, and other food ingredients. The benefits of GM crops, such as higher yields and reduced pesticide use, contribute to the cost-effectiveness and sustainability of food production.

The feed sector is another major application of genetically modified foods. GM crops, particularly corn and soybeans, are used extensively in animal feed. The higher yields and improved nutritional content of GM crops make them an attractive option for livestock producers, as they can lead to better animal health and productivity. Additionally, the reduced need for chemical inputs in GM crop production can result in lower feed costs, benefiting the livestock industry.

In the industrial sector, genetically modified crops are used in the production of various bio-based products. For example, GM corn is used in the production of biofuels such as ethanol, which is a renewable alternative to fossil fuels. The use of GM crops in industrial applications is driven by the need for sustainable and cost-effective raw materials. The versatility of GM crops in producing a wide range of industrial products highlights their potential to contribute to the growth of the bioeconomy.

Other applications of genetically modified foods include pharmaceuticals and bioremediation. GM crops can be engineered to produce pharmaceutical compounds, such as vaccines and therapeutic proteins, offering a cost-effective and scalable alternative to traditional production methods. In bioremediation, genetically modified plants can be used to clean up contaminated environments, such as soil and water, by breaking down pollutants or absorbing heavy metals. These emerging applications demonstrate the potential for genetically modified foods to address a wide range of societal challenges beyond agriculture.

The diverse applications of genetically modified foods underscore their importance in various sectors of the economy. As research and development efforts continue, it is likely that new applications will be discovered, further expanding the market and driving growth. The versatility and adaptability of genetically modified crops make them a valuable tool in addressing the challenges of food security, sustainability, and environmental protection.

## Distribution Channel Analysis

The distribution of genetically modified foods is segmented into supermarkets/hypermarkets, online stores, specialty stores, and others. Supermarkets and hypermarkets are the primary distribution channels, as they offer a wide range of food products, including those made from genetically modified crops. These retail outlets provide consumers with convenient access to GM foods, and their extensive reach makes them a critical component of the distribution network. The presence of GM foods in supermarkets and hypermarkets also helps to normalize their consumption and build consumer trust.

Online stores are another important distribution channel for genetically modified foods. The rise of e-commerce has made it easier for consumers to access a wide range of food products, including those made from GM crops. Online stores offer the convenience of home delivery and often provide detailed product information, which can help to educate consumers about the benefits of GM foods. The growth of online retail is expected to continue, driven by increasing internet penetration and changing consumer preferences, making it an important channel for the distribution of genetically modified foods.

Specialty stores, such as health food stores and organic markets, also play a role in the distribution of genetically modified foods. While these stores are typically associated with non-GMO and organic products, there is a growing recognition of the benefits of GM crops, such as improved nutritional content and reduced environmental impact. As consumer awareness and acceptance of genetically modified foods increase, specialty stores may expand their offerings to include GM products, particularly those with specific health or environmental benefits.

Other distribution channels for genetically modified foods include direct sales, farmers' markets, and cooperatives. Direct sales, such as farm-to-consumer programs, allow farmers to sell their GM crops directly to consumers, providing a more transparent and traceable food supply chain. Farmers' markets and cooperatives offer similar benefits, connecting consumers with local producers and promoting the consumption of locally grown GM foods. These channels can help to build consumer trust and support local economies.

The diverse distribution channels for genetically modified foods highlight the importance of accessibility and consumer education in driving market growth. As the availability of GM foods expands across various retail outlets, it is likely that consumer acceptance will continue to grow, further supporting the market. The integration of genetically modified foods into mainstream distribution channels is a key factor in their widespread adoption and success.

## Opportunities & Threats

The genetically modified food market presents significant opportunities for growth and innovation. One of the primary opportunities lies in the development of new genetically modified crops with enhanced traits. As research and development in genetic engineering and biotechnology continue to advance, there is potential to create crops that are more resilient to climate change, have improved nutritional content, and require fewer resources. These innovations can address critical global challenges such as food security, malnutrition, and environmental sustainability. Additionally, the commercialization of new GM crops can open up new markets and drive economic growth in the agricultural sector.

Another opportunity in the genetically modified food market is the expansion of applications beyond traditional food and feed. For example, GM crops can be used in the production of biofuels, bioplastics, and pharmaceuticals. These emerging applications offer new revenue streams and can contribute to the growth of the bioeconomy. Furthermore, the use of GM crops in bioremediation and other environmental applications can help address ecological challenges and promote sustainable development. The diversification of applications highlights the versatility of genetically modified crops and their potential to drive innovation across various industries.

However, the genetically modified food market also faces several threats and restrainers. One of the primary challenges is public perception and consumer acceptance. Despite the proven benefits of genetically modified crops, there is still significant skepticism and opposition from certain consumer groups and advocacy organizations. Concerns about the safety, environmental impact, and ethical implications of GM foods can hinder market growth and adoption. Addressing these concerns through transparent communication, rigorous safety assessments, and public education is essential to building consumer trust and acceptance.

## Regional Outlook

The regional outlook for the genetically modified food market varies significantly across different parts of the world. North America, particularly the United States, is the leading market for genetically modified foods. The region has a long history of adopting GM crops, driven by favorable government policies, extensive research and development, and strong support from the agricultural sector. The United States alone accounts for a significant share of the global GM crop production, with widespread cultivation of GM corn, soybeans, and cotton. The market in North America is expected to continue growing, supported by ongoing innovation and consumer acceptance.

Asia-Pacific is another key region for the genetically modified food market, with countries like China and India leading the adoption of GM crops. The need to enhance food security and agricultural productivity in densely populated countries is a major driving factor for the market. China, in particular, has made significant investments in biotechnology research and development, leading to the commercialization of various GM crops. The market in Asia-Pacific is expected to grow at a robust CAGR, driven by increasing government support, rising demand for high-yield crops, and growing awareness of the benefits of GM foods.

Europe presents a more complex regional outlook for the genetically modified food market. The region has stringent regulations and a high level of public skepticism regarding GM foods. Countries like Germany, France, and Austria have banned the cultivation of certain GM crops, and the European Union has strict approval processes for GM products. However, there is a gradual shift towards acceptance, driven by ongoing research and positive outcomes from other regions. The market in Europe is expected to grow more slowly compared to North America and Asia-Pacific, but increasing investment in biotechnology and changing consumer perceptions could support future growth.

## Crop Type Analysis

The genetically modified food market is segmented by crop type into corn, soybean, cotton, canola, and others. Among these, corn holds a significant share of the market due to its widespread use in food products, animal feed, and biofuels. Genetically modified corn is engineered to resist pests and tolerate herbicides, which increases its yield and reduces the need for chemical interventions. This has made it highly popular among farmers, particularly in the United States and Brazil, which are leading producers of GM corn.

Soybean is another dominant crop in the GM food market. Genetically modified soybeans are primarily designed for herbicide tolerance, which allows farmers to control weeds more effectively without damaging the crop. This has resulted in higher yields and lower production costs, making GM soybeans a preferred choice for farmers in countries like the United States, Argentina, and Brazil. Additionally, GM soybeans are extensively used in the production of animal feed, which further drives their demand.

Cotton is the third major crop in the genetically modified food market. GM cotton is engineered to be resistant to pests such as the bollworm, which has historically caused significant crop losses. The introduction of GM cotton has led to increased yields and reduced the need for chemical pesticides, thereby lowering the environmental impact of cotton farming. India and China are among the largest producers of GM cotton, contributing significantly to the global market.

Canola is another important crop in the genetically modified food market. GM canola is primarily engineered for herbicide tolerance, which helps in weed management and leads to higher yields. Canada is the largest producer of GM canola, and the crop is extensively used in the production of canola oil, which is a popular cooking oil. The growing demand for canola oil in various food products, coupled with the benefits of GM canola, is expected to drive the market further.

Other crops in the GM food market include fruits and vegetables like potatoes, tomatoes, and papayas, which have been genetically modified to resist diseases and improve shelf life. While these crops constitute a smaller segment compared to corn, soybean, cotton, and canola, they are gradually gaining acceptance as the benefits of genetic modification become more widely recognized.

## Trait Analysis

The genetically modified food market is also segmented by trait into herbicide tolerance, insect resistance, stacked traits, and others. Herbicide tolerance is one of the most common traits engineered into GM crops. Herbicide-tolerant crops can survive the application of specific herbicides that kill weeds, allowing for more efficient weed control. This trait is particularly beneficial for large-scale farming operations, where weed management is a significant challenge. It also reduces the need for tilling, which can help preserve soil structure and reduce erosion.

Insect resistance is another critical trait in the genetically modified food market. Crops with insect-resistant traits produce proteins that are toxic to specific pests but safe for human consumption and other non-target organisms. This reduces the need for chemical pesticides, which can have harmful environmental and health effects. Bt corn and Bt cotton are examples of insect-resistant GM crops that have been widely adopted around the world. The success of these crops has demonstrated the potential for genetically engineered insect resistance to improve crop yields and reduce environmental impacts.

Stacked traits refer to GM crops that possess multiple engineered traits, such as both herbicide tolerance and insect resistance. These crops offer the combined benefits of the individual traits, providing farmers with more comprehensive solutions to the challenges of pest and weed management. Stacked trait crops are particularly popular in regions with diverse agricultural challenges, as they offer a more versatile and effective approach to crop management. The adoption of stacked trait crops is expected to continue growing as more combinations of beneficial traits are developed.

Other traits in the genetically modified food market include drought tolerance, improved nutritional content, and disease resistance. Drought-tolerant crops are engineered to withstand periods of low water availability, which is increasingly important in the face of climate change and the growing scarcity of water resources. Crops with improved nutritional content are engineered to contain higher levels of essential nutrients, such as vitamins and minerals, addressing issues of malnutrition. Disease-resistant crops are engineered to be less susceptible to specific plant diseases, reducing the need for chemical treatments and improving yields.

The growing diversity of traits being engineered into GM crops highlights the potential for continued innovation in the genetically modified food market. As research and development efforts expand, it is likely that new traits will be discovered and commercialized, further enhancing the benefits of genetically modified crops and driving market growth.

## Application Analysis

The application of genetically modified foods extends across various sectors, including food, feed, industrial, and others. The food sector is the most significant application segment, as GM crops are used extensively in the production of various food products. For example, genetically modified corn and soybeans are used in processed foods, cooking oils, and other food ingredients. The benefits of GM crops, such as higher yields and reduced pesticide use, contribute to the cost-effectiveness and sustainability of food production.

The feed sector is another major application of genetically modified foods. GM crops, particularly corn and soybeans, are used extensively in animal feed. The higher yields and improved nutritional content of GM crops make them an attractive option for livestock producers, as they can lead to better animal health and productivity.
